info.text: As the name implies, the HTML video element embeds videos on our page. Like the img and audio elements, you supply the file to load with the src attribute.
info.title: getting started
controls and loop
info.text: In addition to the src attribute, this first video element also contains the controls attribute, so the browser renders its default playback UI (user interface). It also has the loop attribute set, which makes a video restart automatically when it reaches the end.

info.title: boolean attributes
info.text: Unlike the src attribute, controls and loop don't have any values assigned. That's because these are "boolean attributes", which means that their value can either be "true" or "false". Simply writing the attribute's name states that you want it set, where as leaving it absent means you do not.

info.title: source element
info.text: Unlike the img element, the video element has both an opening tag and a closing tag. This is because it can contain a set of optional source elements within them. Notice how this video element has three source elements each with their own src and type attributes.

info.title: media types
info.text: Each source points to the same visual content encoded in a different format, denoted by their different file extensions and MIME type. The browser scans the list and picks the first format it knows how to decode. The first option is an .mp4 which uses H.264 compression, the .ogv which uses Theora, .webm which uses VP9.

info.title: Web Standards
info.text: As we've discussed in our lesson on The Platform, the Web itself is built on open standards rather than owned by any single company. These standards, like HTML, are developed by the W3C, a group where anyone can participate and help shape the language. This process is open and democratic, sometimes slow or messy, but it’s what ensures the Web belongs to everyone.

info.title: historical trace
info.text: The source element is a historical reminder of how messy, but also democratic, the process of making web standards can be. In the early days of HTML5 video, browser vendors couldn’t agree on a single codec, so web developers were told to provide multiple sources in different formats. That way, each browser could pick the format it supported. Today MP4 is nearly universal (consult the compatibility tables on caniuse.com to confirm this) but source remains as a trace of those debates and of the compromises that move the open web forward.
